text: Okay well I've gotten a lot of questions about how we place our ports for a standard robotic lobectomy.
So here you're seeing a right-sided case and I put the first port in the eighth intercostal space.
It's always in the eighth intercostal space and really that port goes at the highest point of the chest.
It ends up being somewhere between the mid and posterior axillary line but the point here is I really focus less on the axillary lines as I do
having it on the highest midpoint of the chest, really at the top of that convexity of the chest.
That gives you the best view.
Then what I do is I have my assistant who's standing on the anterior side of the patient show me the posterior aspect of the chest wall, and I place my intercostal nerve blocks.

placing approximately 2 to 3 cc's of local anesthetic in each of these interspaces, really focusing on not penetrating the parietal pleura so that the local does not leak out into the pleural space but really stays where you want it.
Then I'm going to place a 12 millimeter port posterior to the camera port, again, in the same inner space, the eighth intercostal space.
I make a point not to spread with a Kelly or a tonsil or anything like that because I feel like the spreading motion just causes bleeding and potentially can injure that nerve and cause pain.
So I just pop in with the electrocautery and then enter.
So again, about another hands width posterior to that 12 port, I'm placing an eight millimeter port
Also in the 8th intercostal space posteriorly.
This anterior port really just goes as far forward as I can get it in the 8th inner space where the chest wall meets the diaphragm.
And I'm okay opening a little bit of those diaphragmatic fibers as you see us doing there.
So this can come a little bit through the diaphragm.
That's fine.
So it's all in the 8th intercostal space.
Now here's the assistant port.
The assistant port goes down as low as you can get it, about the 10th or 11th interspace.
And this port I do not like having near the diaphragm fibers.
I want to make sure that it is off the diaphragm because the assistant's going to be taking specimens, perhaps lymph nodes or even a wedge resection or something like that, out through this area.
And you don't want to inadvertently have them get underneath the diaphragm.
So I stay above the diaphragm there.
You can see we are using the air seal port but you can use any air insufflator.
I do like using air seal.
Here we're coming in from the left side of the bed so we always are docking thoracic left and I have those crosshairs line up exactly on my camera port.
I do not target and I'll show you in a moment how we successfully dock without targeting but I find this to be more efficient and effective than targeting.

Each of these arms is docked onto the robot, and then under direct thoracoscopic vision through the assistant port, I'm making sure that the remote center, which is the thick black line on the port, is sitting in the interspace.
Each of the clearances, each clearance joint on each arm gets maximally cleared, so we drop those joints as far down as they can go.
That's the clearance function.
And then we go ahead and put the instruments under direct vision and make sure the remote centers are where we want them to be.
Next, I'm going to show actually a separate case, a left-sided case, to have you understand where to position the arms in relationship to each other.
All right, so the point is that you don't have to target.
If you have this camera arm, arm number two in this case, because we're doing left-sided case, this camera arm, if you have this upper arm, this bar, parallel to the long axis of the patient's body.
If this is way over here, you're going to be torn off.
But you put this arm first, you put this parallel to the long axis of the patient's body, and then every other arm
is in relation to number two.
You want them about two fists apart than I'm talking about up here, okay?
So that's it.
All right, good.
